title: Understanding RCD, MCB, and RCBO protection in a consumer unit
subtitle: What each device does, how to tell them apart, and when each type of fault trips which device
excerpt: A reading guide to the protective devices in a domestic UK consumer unit. Explains what an MCB, RCD, and RCBO does, how to identify which device has tripped and why, and what to do when a circuit trips repeatedly.
sourceNotes: BS 7671: Requirements for Electrical Installations (IET Wiring Regulations 18th Edition). NICEIC consumer guides on circuit protection (publicly available). IET On-Site Guide 18th Edition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A resettable switch that protects an individual circuit from overcurrent (overload) and short-circuit faults. An MCB trips when too much current is drawn — for example, when a motor burns out or a cable is cut. It does not protect against electric shock from earth leakage (that is the RCD's job). MCBs are rated in amps: 6A for lighting, 16A for a radial socket circuit, 32A for a ring main, 40A+ for a cooker.

A device that detects a difference between the current flowing out on the live conductor and the current returning on the neutral — the difference is the leakage current going to earth (potentially through a person). An RCD trips in milliseconds when it detects as little as 30 mA of leakage, which is below the threshold likely to cause cardiac arrest. An RCD does not protect against overcurrent.

glossaryTerms.term: RCBO (Residual Current Breaker with Overcurrent protection)
glossaryTerms.definition: A combined MCB and RCD in a single device. An RCBO protects an individual circuit against both overcurrent (like an MCB) and earth leakage (like an RCD). Used in split-board consumer units where each circuit has individual RCD protection rather than sharing a common RCD with other circuits. When an RCBO trips, only the circuit it protects is affected.

content.text: A modern UK consumer unit contains three types of protective device. Each one does a different job. Knowing which device does what lets you diagnose a tripped circuit correctly. It also tells you when to call an electrician, rather than simply resetting and hoping.

is a single-width breaker protecting one circuit. When it trips, the switch moves to the off or mid position. Cause: the circuit was overloaded (too many appliances drawing current at once) or a short circuit occurred (live touched neutral or earth). An

is a wider device that protects a group of circuits. When it trips, all circuits sharing that RCD lose power simultaneously. Cause: earth leakage; a fault to earth somewhere in the group of circuits, or a faulty appliance drawing current to earth. An
protects one circuit against both overcurrent and earth leakage. When it trips, only that one circuit loses power, and the cause can be either an overload or a leakage fault.

Open the consumer unit cover (do not touch any conductors inside). Look for the device in the off or mid position. If a single MCB is down, you have an overload or short circuit on that specific circuit. If the wide RCD is down, you have an earth leakage fault on one of the circuits in its group. If an RCBO is down, it is both breaker and RCD in one; one circuit is affected.

When an RCD trips, systematically identify the faulty circuit. With the RCD still off, switch all MCBs in the RCD group to off. Reset the RCD. Switch the MCBs back on one at a time: the RCD will trip again when you turn on the MCB connected to the faulty circuit. That circuit has an earth leakage fault; unplug all appliances on that circuit and reset again. If the RCD holds, one of the unplugged appliances has a leakage fault: test them one at a time. If the RCD trips with no appliances connected, the fixed wiring has a fault; instruct an electrician.

An MCB that trips repeatedly on the same circuit may be undersized for the load. An RCD that trips for no clear reason (nuisance tripping) may be picking up very small leakage from several appliances combined. Old cooker elements, washing machine heater elements, and submersible pump motors are common sources. An electrician can measure the total leakage current before the RCD is condemned.

Every RCD and RCBO has a test button. Press it once a month with all circuits on: the device should trip immediately. If it does not trip when the test button is pressed, the device has failed and must be replaced by an electrician; a non-tripping RCD provides no shock protection.
